items.title: Symmetrical distal diabetic polyneuropathy
items.contentHtml:
Distal symmetrical sensory-motor diabetic neuropathy

  • It is the most common form of diabetic neuropathy, with a mixed pathogenesis (metabolic and microvascular).
  • It presents with distal sensory loss in a glove and sock pattern, with late motor involvement and abolition of the Achilles reflex as a sign. early.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; It is confirmed with electromyography and conduction velocity which shows sensory-motor axonal neuropathy.
  • First-line treatment is strict glycemic control; Neuropathic pain responds to gabapentin or pregabalin.

items.foco: Diabetic mononeuropathy of the third cranial nerve
items.title: Third nerve paralysis due to diabetes
items.contentHtml:
Diabetic mononeuropathy of the III cranial nerve

  • It is a cranial mononeuropathy that spares the pupil (key difference with posterior communicating aneurysm).
  • It presents with ptosis, ophthalmoplegia and diplopia, but the pupillary reflex remains intact due to preferential involvement of somatic motor fibers.
  • The pathophysiology is ischemic. microvascular; It usually reverts spontaneously in 3-6 months.
  • On examination, the Tinel sign is negative (not applicable in cranial entrapment); The differential diagnosis includes neuropathy due to tumor compression.

items.foco: Median nerve entrapment in carpal tunnel
items.title: carpal tunnel syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Entrapment of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel

  • Compression of the median nerve at the level of the flexor retinaculum; common in diabetes due to greater susceptibility.
  • Clinic: paresthesias in the first three fingers and the radial half of the fourth, which worsen at night; Positive Tinel sign (percussion over the tunnel reproduces symptoms). if it fails, infiltration with corticosteroids; release surgery if refractory.

items.title: Ulnar canal syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Entrapment of the ulnar nerve in the elbow

  • Compression of the ulnar nerve in the epitrochleo-olecranon canal; It causes paresthesias in the fourth and fifth fingers (ulnar border).
  • Signs: Tinel's sign positive at the elbow, weakness of the thumb-index clamp (Froment's sign) and interosseous atrophy.
  • In electromyography and conduction velocity a slowing of the speed of motor conduction through the elbow is observed.
  • Treatment: avoid prolonged support on elbow, night splint in extension; if not improved, surgical decompression.

items.title: Diabetic gastrointestinal dysautonomia
items.contentHtml:
Gastrointestinal diabetic autonomic neuropathy

  • Affects the autonomic nervous system; the most common manifestation is diabetic gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying).
  • Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, postprandial fullness, abdominal distention; It can alternate with nocturnal diarrhea and steatorrhea.
  • Diagnosis: gastric emptying scan with retention >60% at 2 hours.
  • First-line treatment: gabapentin is not indicated; Prokinetics (metoclopramide, domperidone) and glycemic control are used.

items.foco: Loss of the Achilles reflex in diabetic neuropathy
items.title: Achilles reflex abolished
items.contentHtml:
Loss of the Achilles reflex in diabetic neuropathy

  • It is one of the earliest signs of distal symmetrical sensory-motor diabetic neuropathy.
  • The abolition of the Achilles reflex reflects the involvement of proprioceptive and motor fibers of the posterior tibial nerve.
  • It is explored with the patient kneeling or in a prone position, percussing the Achilles tendon; The normal response is plantar flexion of the foot.
  • Unilateral loss requires ruling out S1 radiculopathy or entrapment of the posterior tibial nerve in the tarsus.

items.foco: Difference between polyneuropathy and mononeuropathy multiplex
items.title: Polyneuropathy vs mononeuropathy multiplex
items.contentHtml:
Difference between polyneuropathy and mononeuropathy multiplex

  • Polineneuropathy: symmetrical, distal and diffuse involvement of peripheral nerves, usually sensory-motor (e.g. distal symmetric diabetic neuropathy).
  • Mononeuropathy multiplex: asymmetric involvement of two or more non-contiguous nerve trunks, typical of vasculitis, diabetes or leprosy.
  • In diabetes, mononeuropathy multiplex can present as diabetic mononeuropathy of the III cranial nerve together with entrapment of the median.
  • The differential diagnosis is supported by electromyography and conduction velocity: polyneuropathy shows diffuse axonal pattern; mononeuropathy multiplex, focal lesions.

items.foco: Common peroneal nerve and foot drop
items.title: Common peroneal palsy
items.contentHtml:
Common peroneal nerve and foot drop

  • The common peroneal nerve is compressed at the head of the fibula; causes foot drop (inability to dorsiflexion and eversion of the foot).
  • Gait is steppage (exaggerated elevation of the hip to avoid dragging the foot).
  • The Tinel sign is positive to percussion on the head of the fibula.
  • In electromyography and conduction velocity a slowing of speed is observed. motor conduction of the common peroneus with denervation in the tibialis anterior.
  • Treatment: avoid compression, anti-equinus orthosis; if not improved, surgical decompression.

items.foco: Posterior tibial nerve entrapment in tarsus
items.title: Tarsal tunnel syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Entrapment of the posterior tibial nerve in the tarsus

  • Compression of the posterior tibial nerve in the flexor retinaculum of the ankle (tarsal tunnel).
  • Clinic: pain and paresthesias in the sole of the foot (toes and heel), which worsen with walking and at night; Tinel sign positive retro internal malleolar.
  • Differential diagnosis with distal symmetrical sensory-motor diabetic neuropathy (the latter is bilateral and symmetrical).
  • electromyography and conduction velocity shows prolonged distal motor latency of the posterior tibial nerve.
  • Treatment: night splint, anti-inflammatories; if unsuccessful, release surgery.

items.foco: Painful diabetic neuropathy and gabapentin treatment
items.title: Diabetic neuropathic pain and gabapentin
items.contentHtml:
Painful diabetic neuropathy and treatment with gabapentin

  • Neuropathic pain in diabetes is burning, stabbing or lancinating, with allodynia and hyperalgesia.
  • gabapentin is a first-line drug for neuropathic pain; It is started at a dose of 300 mg/day and titrated up to 1800-3600 mg/day divided into three doses.
  • Alternatives: pregabalin, amitriptyline, duloxetine; gabapentin has a lower profile of anticholinergic effects than tricyclic antidepressants.
  • Before starting, evaluate renal function (creatinine clearance <60 mL/min requires dose adjustment of gabapentin).

items.title: Tinel sign
items.contentHtml:
Positive Tinel sign in entrapments

  • The Tinel sign consists of the reproduction of paresthesias or pain in the territory of the nerve when percussing the point of compression.
  • It is positive in entrapments such as carpal tunnel syndrome (medium), ulnar canal syndrome (ulnar), syndrome of the tarsal tunnel (posterior tibial) and compression of the common peroneal nerve.
  • Its sensitivity is limited (~50-60%); a false positive can occur in diabetic neuropathy without entrapment.
  • It is complemented by electromyography and conduction velocity to confirm the exact site of the lesion.

items.foco: Evaluation with electromyography and conduction velocity
items.title: Electromyography and conduction velocities
items.contentHtml:
Evaluation with electromyography and conduction velocity

  • electromyography and conduction velocity is the gold standard for differentiating axonal neuropathy from demyelinating neuropathy and localizing entrapments.
  • In distal sensory-motor symmetric diabetic neuropathy a decrease in the amplitude of the action potential (axonal) is observed with normal or slightly normal conduction velocity reduced.
  • In entrapments (carpal tunnel, ulnar elbow, tarsus) focal slowing of motor or sensory conduction velocity is evident through the compression site.
  • electromyography and conduction velocity also rules out polyradiculopathies or hereditary neuropathies.
